text: Despite much effort, direct catalytic conversion of methane to chemical commodities is highly inefficient and remains a grand challenge. The current production of acetic acid using transition metal homogeneous catalysts based on the high energy input of the catalytic carbonylation of methanol process is energetically inefficient and highly unsustainable. We have developed a new, room temperature water-mediated, photo-activation catalyst that can directly catalyse the conversion of CH4 and CO2 to give a solution of high concentration of acetic acid under illumination. A TiO2 supported ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate anchored with isolated Pt single atoms (Pt1@NPW/TiO2) shows the highest yield of acetic acid with a solution concentration as high as 5.7 mmol·L-1 in 60 h with selectivity > 90% and 66% on liquid and carbon basis, respectively. The catalyst gives an unparalleled productivity of 99 acetic acid moles per mole of Pt. Using combined isotopic labelling experiments and in situ bulk and surface 13C NMR, we show that the aqueous photo-activation of the high performance catalyst yields acetic acid via a catalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane process centred about the Pt1 sites, in which the activation of methane is highly enhanced in the presence of hydroxyl species derived from water.
